summary: When raising Convoy's seed round, Dan Lewis found that getting his engineering team formally onboard well before the raise mattered more than almost anything else in the pitch. Having five or six engineers already committed and working on the product, with their names and photos on the fundraising deck, and their willingness to serve as references for investors, materially changed how investors received the pitch. This works because it de-risks the technical-execution question for investors who often can't independently evaluate engineering quality themselves - seeing experienced engineers already bought in and vouching for the idea is a stronger signal than the founder's own pitch.
quote: we had five or six people working on this who were engineers and helping us when we wanted to go raise money that was the thing that made all the difference the other stuff I mentioned is super important having the engineering team doing it and committed and like their names on a slide and their pictures and like them willing to be references changed it so that was that was pretty big

summary: Lewis pitched investors on a spectrum from "binary/nonlinear" businesses that must reach massive scale or effectively fail - he cites gaming and social networking/messaging, noting that even a company like Path that reached medium scale still didn't really succeed - to "linear" businesses where effort predictably converts into return. He argued trucking/logistics is linear: you can put in the energy, do the homework, and expect a level of return, rather than needing a lucky viral spark to get off the ground. He used this framing explicitly to differentiate Convoy from other deals investors were considering, arguing it made Convoy both the safer and the smarter bet - a useful angle specifically because many investors lack the domain expertise to independently judge social networking versus gaming versus trucking.
quote: there are a lot of businesses that really aren't going to be successful unless they're massive unless they really nail it... whereas there are other businesses that are much more linear and effort and I think they're less risky... you don't see a lot of like mid-sized social networks like path right kind of was medium sized and it's done it's done it has to be massive or it doesn't really go

summary: Convoy's founders explicitly ruled out bootstrapping because they believed trucking had just hit a narrow technology-enabled window: truck driver smartphone penetration, reliable data plans, and accurate GPS (even in rural areas) had only recently become widespread enough to make incumbents vulnerable to disruption. Because it was a marketplace business, they believed first-mover advantage and speed to market mattered enormously, so they treated time as directly convertible into money - raising capital was the way to buy the speed needed to capture the window before someone else did, rather than growing slowly on bootstrapped revenue.
quote: we looked at this business as hey technology just shifted truck drivers didn't have smartphones two years ago... because technology allowed that to happen it actually in our business it's going to fundamentally change the way the entire business can be constructed... so we basically thought time equals money to accelerate us getting to market

summary: Lewis compares picking investors to dating: choose people you genuinely enjoy talking to and feel natural chemistry with, because the moment they sign the first check is one of the best moments that relationship will ever have. From there the relationship generally gets harder, so the openness and trust built early is what founders end up leaning on later when things aren't going well - not something that can be built after the fact, once it's actually needed.
quote: you've got to pick the right investors you have to like actually it's dating like when you meet the person you really want to actually enjoy the conversation you have with them and feel like there's some natural relationship and chemistry there because you're in the best of times when you're raising money from them for the first time... it only gets harder from the time they're signing that first check

summary: Right after closing Convoy's seed round, some of its own investors advised Lewis to immediately write the Series B deck, even though the company hadn't yet raised its Series A - the logic being that if you believe you'll eventually raise that later round, you may never actually get there unless you define the story for it now. Convoy applied the same logic after the seed: they worked backward from what the Series A story needed to say (customer diversity across categories, enough shipment volume to understand the different permutations, key team gaps filled) to figure out exactly which milestones to hit before raising, rather than raising first and figuring out the story later.
quote: some of the best advice I got even when we were raising our series A as soon as we were done some of our investors when I was talking about it they said write your series B deck right now like if you believe you're going to do a series B you may never do it

summary: To build toward Convoy's Series A story, Lewis drew a two-by-two grid mapping the credibility areas the company needed: understanding the demand side of the marketplace, the supply side, marketplace dynamics generally, and the trucking industry itself. He used the grid as a live recruiting tracker, writing the name of every candidate he was interviewing into the corresponding box, and built the operations team in parallel with the product over one summer so that by the September launch - and by the time he pitched Series A investors - he had people in place who made the company's story credible in exactly the areas where he personally lacked deep experience.
quote: I actually drew a grid it was like a two by two I was like I need to have people who understand you know demand side of the business in a marketplace the supply part of business in the marketplace really understand marketplace dynamics and really understand the trucking industry... I took every person I was speaking with during my recruiting during the summer... and we wrote everyone's name that we were recruiting on that sheet

summary: Instead of defining company values at founding, Convoy's first five engineers and Lewis held a values retreat in week one and deliberately concluded they didn't yet know who they were as a company, so they paused the exercise entirely. About a year later, once the company had grown to roughly 30 people and Lewis could still see and talk to everyone end to end, they revisited it - this time by asking what behaviors they were already doing that were working and wanted to keep, rather than aspirationally inventing values from nothing. This retrospective, evidence-based approach to defining culture runs counter to the common startup advice to nail down values on day one.
quote: we first started the company the first five engineers and me and Grant went to like a retreat for a weekend this is week one just to kind of talk about who we wanted to be as a company and we started thinking about values and goals and we kind of said we don't know who we are yet right we paused that and then a year later we came back to it and said what allowed us to achieve these things

summary: One of Convoy's core values is "know why," which Lewis explains exists to enable employees to exercise independent judgment and handle situations not explicitly covered by instructions. His reasoning: if people only follow directives without understanding the underlying reason, they get stuck the moment circumstances change or the decision-maker isn't around to ask; understanding why also makes people more fulfilled at work because they can act autonomously instead of needing to be told what to do in every new situation.
quote: know why is one of the things that plays into that because I don't think you can exercise independent judgment and you can't go off script and you can't handle exceptions and things like that if you don't know why you're doing something so I never want to have a culture where people are like well we're doing because Dan said so

summary: Before starting Convoy, Lewis ran a structured idea-validation process: first extensive desk research (industry reports, competitor material), then forcing himself to write out every possible customer type in the industry and what each currently pays money for (trucks, software, advice, etc.), combining those into several candidate business concepts written up as short user-story-style paragraphs. Only after that did he go have open-ended field conversations with industry people, using the pre-written concepts to give the conversations enough shape to be useful without being overwhelming. That process produced three genuinely distinct trucking business ideas, none of which matched what he had originally written down - the field conversations reshaped the ideas rather than simply confirming them.
quote: I actually did a bunch of research first right I went on the internet and learned everything I could about it... I forced myself to basically write down like who are the customers who are all the different customer options... and then wrote actual like paragraph or two... and so it didn't turn out to be any of the things we wrote down but there were actually three distinct businesses we came up with within trucking
